optimist.name: Wendy Okonkwo
optimist.column:
The Nationals lost yesterday, but here’s the thing: they’re still in the game. Not just in the standings, but in the narrative of a rebuild that’s supposed to take years, maybe even a decade, to bear fruit. And let’s be real—this team isn’t just chasing a .500 record. They’re chasing something bigger, something that feels like it’s been waiting in the dirt under the infield since the last playoff run. I’ve got a vial of that dirt in my desk drawer, and it smells like hope.  

You know what’s fascinating about this team? They’re not just stacking prospects; they’re figuring out how to make them work. Elly Wood’s line drives have a physics of their own—sometimes they go for a double, sometimes they get caught, but they always make you lean in. That’s the beauty of a rebuild. It’s not about the wins right now, it’s about the moments when a kid with a fastball and a dream starts to understand the game. You can’t teach that kind of grit. You can only watch it unfold.  

The 3.5-game deficit in the NL East? That’s not a death sentence. It’s a reminder that baseball is a long season, and even the best teams stumble.

pessimist.name: Murray Feldschneider
pessimist.column:
The Nationals lost again. Another 1-6 drubbing, another reminder that this team isn’t just struggling—it’s unraveling. The Cardinals didn’t just beat them; they embarrassed them, dismantling a lineup that’s supposed to be the foundation of a playoff contender. You can’t blame the fans for wondering if this is a new era of futility or just a continuation of the same old nightmare. The Lerners sold a World Series and bought a minor-league reunion tour. This team has more gift-shop hats than wins in May.  

The real tragedy isn’t the loss—it’s the way it’s happening. The Nationals’ pitching staff, once a glimmer of hope, now looks like a collection of misfits. Every start feels like a audition for a minor-league gig, with relievers walking batters and starters crumbling under the weight of expectations. You’d think a team with a payroll that could buy a small country would have a pitching staff that doesn’t resemble a college team on a budget. Instead, we get a parade of injuries, poor decisions, and a bullpen that’s more prone to collapse than a bridge in New Orleans.  

And let’s not forget the offense. The bats, supposed to be the backbone of this rebuild, are flailing. They’ve scored fewer runs than the Cardinals in their last three games, and the frustration is palpable. You can’t help but wonder if the front office is so desperate to chase the past that
